text: many arts the art of legerdemain and illusion suffers a slow drift from origins as a spiritual technique into an abused form of popular entertainment and a malignant tool in the hands of skeptics. What happens if we take Keel’s approach to the tools of a hoax, and understand its potency in creating real effects? What happens when the tools of the debunker are admitted, but instead of being bitterly thrust forward to expose the immediate hoax, they are rather pushed further to reveal more profound mysteries? It’s a mistake to think that mystery disappears when one understands the illusion. Theater, poetry, sculpture, painting, all forms of artifice have their basis in ritual. As Rachael Blyth, of the ritualtheater ensemble Foolish People, points out in her exploration of ritual media “Inside the Temple of Cinema,” even modified by contemporary technology, the sacred root of art remains approachable to those who know where to find it. The same goes for a good story, for an unexplainable moment of experience can only find reality when couched in a strange intermixture of material fact and imaginal proposition. Philosophy, when fully expressed, encompasses all aspects of life. A relationship with wisdom provides the basis for everything that follows, and properly aligned, all experience and action come under its guidance. Something such as sleight of hand, the ability to manipulate perception, is a powerful spiritual technique when given the right spin. It does well enough as a tool for skeptics to peel back the layers of mystery, but why stop at the flesh when you can reach well beyond the heart. Laboratory results in experiments with Y (psi, a term used for a broad range of paranormal phenomena) often reveal a very interesting form of the observer effect, demonstrating correlations between positive reinforcement and belief and gaining results. This can be observed in the placebo effect as well, where belief becomes, in some ways, the basic motivating factor of success. This is often used to counter miraculous claims without ever noticing that, in themselves, both of these tendencies are a bit miraculous. ILLUMINATION AND THE PROPER USE OF STAGE MAGIC When seeking an explanation for phenomena, or a straight materialist cause/effect scenario, the observer effect and placebos don’t provide an adequate basis for a proper scientific model. However, if we seek to understand and utilize these factors in our lives, no model is necessary, merely a grasp of the situational and atmospheric requirements to induce the phenomenon. The fact that devotees of Elvis report miraculous healing shows the power of a charismatic stage presence goes a long way. When that presence is guided by sound philosophy, this power can be used to move beyond selling albums into more refined realms of social change. Imagine the ability for a full expression of art, one that moves beyond the separate practices of visual arts, music, poetry, and dance, and that combines them with a mastery of perception. THE MIRACLE OF SUGAR During the early twentieth century, Harlan Tarbell, a close friend of Harry Houdini, developed one of the most enduring courses on stage magic. A unique aspect of Tarbell’s course was its focus on physical exercises to encourage greater body control. Similar to preset routines practiced in the martial arts or ballet, these exercises prepare students for future situations and allow muscle memory to take over when the magician’s focus is required elsewhere to direct his or her performance. As with any repeated physical exercise, these routines also aid in a deeper understanding of our interconnection between body and mind, and if extended further, of the spirit. In some ways Tarbell endorsed a sort of yoga for practicing illusionists. It is interesting to note accounts from a visit Tarbell made shortly after World War I to Le Mesnil-Saint-Denis in the north of France: A few years ago, Cagliostro [i.e., Tarbell] came to Husson Castle. He was a stranger, nobody had seen him before, nor knew exactly where he came from and where he went. But he did wonders. One day, Madame Husson had guests for dinner and she said she regretted not being able to serve the sugar with the coffee. Then this strange man, Cagliostro, got up and found in the air, enough sugar for all of the guests. Then he went to the monastery and had compassion for the nuns, orphans and wounded, he raised his hands and produced sugar. Then a hundred pounds of sugar arrived so they were able to retain all the results. The sugar appeared in the air, sir, materialized out of the air. Yes, sir, materialized out of the air. Yes, sir, we saw a miracle. 6 As master magician Jeff McBride teaches, the principles of stage magic require a thorough knowledge of optics, psychology, body control, set, and setting. To perform sleight of hand one must truly be on intimate terms with the ways in which the physical world interacts with the mind. Going farther with this, one could say that to achieve truly lasting results, one must have mastered the very illusion of the world that surrounds us. McBride puts it succinctly when he says, “To make magic, one has to have experienced magic.” One who explores the art of perception gains the ability to create effects in the world by altering the perception of witnesses. Thinking of a stage magician in this way may seem grandiose, but the use that this knowledge has played in debunking fraud and exposing hoaxes shows that such an understanding can be used as a powerful tool for exposing the truth. But as much as it can expose, it can also foster a connection to the truth. When we think of the full potential of this, it becomes obvious that the path of legerdemain leads into very interesting spiritual territory. BITTER MISDIRECTION Fundamentalist skepticism has kept this understanding of altering the perception of witnesses from reaching its full development as a cultural tool.
